raw_response.title: Ensi: AI-Powered Product Development Pipeline MVP

raw_response.executive_summary: Ensi is an AI-powered product development pipeline that automates the journey from a product brief to deployed code. This MVP focuses on establishing the core pipeline, which uses orchestrated LLM agents to transform a product brief into a series of structured, validated development artifacts: a Product Requirements Document (PRD), an Architecture Design, and User Stories. These stories are then automatically pushed as issues into the Multica project management platform. The entire process is managed by a robust Finite State Machine (FSM) built on a Rust backend, leveraging the Google Gemini API for generative tasks.

functional_requirements.description: The system must orchestrate the sequence of AI agent tasks using a Finite State Machine. The FSM will manage state transitions from Brief -> PRD -> Architecture -> Stories -> Code.
The system must provide a mechanism for users to input a product brief to initiate a new pipeline run. For the MVP, this will be a command-line interface (CLI) that accepts a text file.

Each agent in the pipeline must generate a structured artifact (PRD, Architecture, Stories, Code). The output of each stage must be validated against a predefined schema before the FSM transitions to the next state.
The system must be able to authenticate with the Multica API and create new issues in a specified project based on the user stories generated by the pipeline.

non_functional_requirements.availability: The Ensi pipeline service will target 99.5% uptime during business hours for the MVP.
performance: Each individual AI agent generation step (e.g., PRD generation) must complete within 120 seconds.
The end-to-end pipeline from brief to stories in Multica should complete in under 10 minutes for a standard feature brief.
Gemini API calls must have a timeout of 90 seconds to prevent indefinite hangs.
scalability: The Rust backend must support at least 10 concurrent pipeline runs without significant performance degradation.
The system should be designed with stateless services where possible to allow for horizontal scaling in the future.
The application must handle API rate limits from external services (Gemini, Multica) gracefully using exponential backoff.
security: All external API keys (Google Gemini, Multica) must be stored securely using environment variables or a dedicated secrets management service.
User-provided briefs and generated artifacts must be handled as confidential data and not be logged publicly or used for external model training.
Access to the pipeline execution environment must be restricted to authorized personnel.

out_of_scope: A graphical user interface (GUI). The MVP will be CLI-only.
Real-time collaborative editing of generated artifacts.
Generation of frontend code (HTML, CSS, JavaScript).
Direct deployment of generated code to any environment (staging, production).
Support for any LLM other than Google Gemini.
Manual approval gates or editing steps within the pipeline run.
raw_response.problem_statement: The traditional software development lifecycle is characterized by slow, manual handoffs between product, design, and engineering teams. This process is prone to errors, inconsistencies, and significant time delays. Translating a high-level brief into actionable, technically sound user stories and code requires substantial manual effort, leading to long cycle times and high overhead even for simple features. Discrepancies between the PRD, architecture, and final implementation are common, resulting in rework and misaligned outcomes.

risks.description: LLM-generated artifacts may be inconsistent, inaccurate, or 'hallucinated', leading to poor quality outputs.
risks.mitigation: Implement strict JSON schema validation for all outputs. Use few-shot prompting with high-quality examples to guide the model. Log all generated outputs for manual review and fine-tuning of prompts.

risks.description: High operational costs or API rate limiting from the Google Gemini API could disrupt service.
risks.mitigation: Implement robust caching for similar requests. Set up strict budget alerts and monitoring in Google Cloud. Optimize prompts to be as token-efficient as possible. Implement graceful degradation if API limits are hit.

risks.description: The quality of the generated code skeletons may be too low to be useful for developers, leading to low adoption.
risks.mitigation: For the MVP, focus on generating well-defined boilerplate, configuration files, and function signatures rather than complex business logic. Automatically run linters and formatters on all generated code to ensure it meets quality standards.

risks.description: Changes in the Multica API could break the integration, preventing stories from being created in the backlog.
risks.mitigation: Develop the Multica integration as a separate, loosely-coupled module with its own set of integration tests. Subscribe to Multica's developer updates and pin the integration to a specific, stable API version.

raw_response.solution_overview: Ensi streamlines and automates the initial phases of the development lifecycle. A user provides a product brief, which triggers an automated pipeline. A series of specialized AI agents, orchestrated by a Pipeline FSM, sequentially generate and validate key artifacts. The 'PM Agent' creates a PRD, the 'Architect Agent' designs the system architecture, and the 'Dev Agent' breaks the work down into user stories. Each artifact's structure is validated before proceeding to the next stage, ensuring consistency and quality. Finally, Ensi integrates with the Multica platform to create issues from the generated stories, seamlessly bridging the gap between planning and execution.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Monitoring the final state of the Pipeline FSM for each run via system logs.
success_metrics.target: 85% of initiated pipelines complete successfully without critical errors.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Calculating the delta between the 'Brief Ingested' and 'Stories Pushed' FSM state timestamps.
Analysis of pipeline initiation logs, aggregated by user.
success_metrics.target: 10 internal users initiating at least 5 pipeline runs per week.

success_metrics.measurement_method: A simple feedback survey presented to the user at the end of each successful pipeline run.
success_metrics.target: Average user rating of 4 out of 5 for the usefulness of generated artifacts.

raw_response.title: Ensi: AI-Powered Product Development Pipeline (MVP)

acceptance_criteria: A CLI command `ensi run --brief <path/to/brief.txt>` exists.
The command successfully triggers the Pipeline FSM, starting in the 'Brief Ingested' state.
The system provides a run ID to the user upon successful initiation.
If the brief file is not found or is empty, the system returns a clear error message.
user_stories.i_want: to initiate a new Ensi pipeline run by providing a product brief via a CLI command
user_stories.so_that: I can begin the automated process of generating all necessary development artifacts.
user_stories.title: Initiate Pipeline Run from Brief

acceptance_criteria: When the FSM is in the 'PRD Generation' state, the PM Agent is invoked with the brief content.
The agent generates a JSON object conforming to the predefined PRD schema.
The generated PRD includes sections for Problem Statement, Target Personas, and Functional Requirements.
The output is validated against the schema before the FSM transitions to the next state.
user_stories.i_want: Ensi to automatically generate a structured PRD in JSON format based on my initial brief
user_stories.so_that: I can save time and ensure all key requirement sections are covered.
user_stories.title: Generate PRD from Brief

acceptance_criteria: When the FSM is in the 'Architecture Generation' state, the Architect Agent is invoked with the PRD.
The agent generates a JSON object describing the proposed architecture.
The architecture document includes key components, data models, and API endpoints.
The output is validated against the architecture schema.
user_stories.i_want: Ensi to generate a high-level architecture design based on the generated PRD
user_stories.so_that: I can quickly establish a technical approach and identify key components.
user_stories.title: Generate Architecture from PRD

acceptance_criteria: When the FSM is in the 'Story Generation' state, the Dev Agent is invoked with the PRD and Architecture documents.
The agent generates an array of user stories in a structured JSON format.
Each story includes a title, role, want, reason, and at least two acceptance criteria.
The output is validated against the user story schema.
user_stories.i_want: Ensi to break down the requirements into a list of actionable user stories

raw_response.executive_summary: Ensi is an AI-powered product development pipeline designed to dramatically accelerate the software development lifecycle. It automates the creation of key development artifacts, transforming a high-level product brief into a structured PRD, technical architecture, user stories, and boilerplate code. By leveraging a series of orchestrated LLM agents governed by a Finite State Machine (FSM), Ensi reduces manual effort, minimizes inconsistencies, and ensures alignment from concept to code. This MVP focuses on validating the core pipeline for a Rust-based backend, integrating with the Multica issue tracking platform.

functional_requirements.description: The system must provide a core orchestration engine based on a Finite State Machine (FSM) to manage the sequential execution of artifact generation agents. The FSM will handle state transitions, inputs/outputs for each stage, and error handling.
The system must use specialized LLM agents to generate a series of structured artifacts: PRD from a brief, Architecture from a PRD, User Stories from Architecture, and Code from User Stories.

At the completion of each generation stage, the system must validate the generated artifact against a predefined JSON schema to ensure its structure and integrity before it is passed to the next stage.

The system must integrate with the Multica issue tracking platform's API to automatically create new issues from the generated user stories, populating fields such as title, description, and acceptance criteria.

For the MVP, users will interact with the Ensi pipeline via a Command Line Interface (CLI). The CLI must support initiating a pipeline run with a product brief and checking the status of an ongoing run.

non_functional_requirements.availability: The Ensi service will target 99.5% uptime during business hours for the MVP.
performance: Each individual agent stage (e.g., PRD generation) must complete in under 3 minutes on average.
The end-to-end pipeline for a medium-sized feature brief should complete in under 15 minutes.
The CLI status command must return a response in under 500ms.
scalability: The Rust backend must be built on an asynchronous framework (e.g., Tokio) to handle at least 10 concurrent pipeline executions.
The application will be containerized using Docker for portability and ease of scaling.
The system should be stateless to allow for horizontal scaling of compute instances.
security: All external API keys (Gemini, Multica) must be stored in a secure vault or as encrypted environment variables, not in source code.
All communication with external APIs must use TLS 1.2 or higher (HTTPS).
Input briefs must be sanitized to prevent prompt injection attacks against the LLM agents.

out_of_scope: A graphical user interface (GUI). All interaction will be via the CLI for the MVP.
Support for code generation in languages other than Rust.
Human-in-the-loop feedback and correction within a single pipeline run. Rerunning the pipeline is the prescribed method for changes.
Generation of frontend code, UI/UX designs, or test cases.
Automated deployment of the generated code to any environment.
raw_response.problem_statement: The traditional software development process is characterized by slow, manual handoffs between product, design, and engineering teams. This leads to significant overhead, context loss, and inconsistencies between requirements, architecture, and implementation. Product managers spend days writing detailed PRDs, architects translate them into designs, and developers break them down into stories, all of which are error-prone and time-consuming. This friction slows down time-to-market and diverts skilled engineers from high-value problem-solving to repetitive, administrative tasks.

risks.description: LLM-generated artifacts (PRD, architecture, code) may contain inaccuracies, logical flaws, or 'hallucinations' that are not caught by schema validation.
risks.mitigation: Implement strict, multi-layered validation. Use few-shot prompting with high-quality examples to guide the LLM. For the MVP, all generated artifacts require mandatory human review and approval before being used.

risks.description: Dependency on the external Gemini API may lead to pipeline failures or slowdowns due to rate limiting, latency, or outages.
risks.mitigation: Implement robust error handling with exponential backoff and retry mechanisms for API calls. Cache results where appropriate. Monitor API performance and have a process to upgrade service tiers if needed.

risks.description: The generated architecture or code, while syntactically correct, may be suboptimal, inefficient, or not aligned with our company's best practices.
risks.mitigation: The Architect and Developer agents will be heavily prompted with our internal architectural principles and coding standards. All generated architecture and code must be reviewed by a senior engineer before implementation.

risks.description: Changes in the Multica API could break the issue creation integration, disrupting the final step of the pipeline.
risks.mitigation: Isolate the Multica API client behind an abstraction layer (anti-corruption layer). Develop a suite of contract tests against a mock Multica server to quickly detect breaking changes.

raw_response.solution_overview: Ensi introduces a fully automated pipeline that orchestrates specialized LLM agents to generate a cascade of development artifacts. The process begins when a user submits a product brief. The 'Pipeline FSM' then triggers a sequence of agents: a PM Agent generates a PRD, an Architect Agent creates a technical design, a Tech Lead Agent decomposes it into user stories, and a Developer Agent generates boilerplate Rust code. Each stage's output is structurally validated against a predefined schema before proceeding to the next, ensuring quality and consistency. For the MVP, generated user stories will be automatically pushed as issues into the Multica platform, creating a ready-to-work backlog.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Timestamp logging at the start and end of the 'Stories Generated' stage within the Pipeline FSM.
Tracking the final state ('Completed' vs 'Failed') of all pipeline runs in the application's database.
success_metrics.target: 85% of pipeline runs complete successfully without validation errors.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Surveys and interviews with the initial cohort of developer users.
success_metrics.target: < 30 minutes per feature (reviewing and adapting generated code)

success_metrics.measurement_method: Tracking the number of unique users initiating pipeline runs.
success_metrics.target: Adoption by at least 2 internal engineering teams within the first quarter.

Generate clear, consistent, and comprehensive requirement documents.
Reduce ambiguity and misinterpretation in the development process.
Focus more on strategic product discovery and less on documentation.

raw_response.executive_summary: Ensi is an AI-powered product development pipeline that automates and accelerates the software development lifecycle. By orchestrating a series of specialized LLM agents within a finite state machine, Ensi transforms a high-level product brief into a series of structured, validated artifacts: a Product Requirements Document (PRD), a technical architecture, user stories, and deployable code. The MVP aims to significantly reduce manual effort, eliminate inconsistencies between development stages, and increase overall development velocity.

functional_requirements.description: The system must orchestrate the generation of artifacts using a Finite State Machine (FSM). The FSM will manage the state transitions from Brief -> PRD -> Architecture -> Stories -> Code.
The system must use LLM agents to generate structured artifacts at each stage of the pipeline. Each agent will be specialized for its specific task (e.g., PRD generation, architecture design).
The output of each pipeline stage must be validated against a predefined schema before the FSM can transition to the next state. Invalid outputs should trigger a retry or error state.

The system must be able to create new issues in a specified Multica project based on the generated user stories. This includes mapping story titles, descriptions, and acceptance criteria to Multica issue fields.

non_functional_requirements.availability: The Ensi service API will maintain 99.5% uptime during business hours for the MVP.
performance: The P95 latency for the initial brief submission API endpoint must be under 500ms.
Each individual AI-powered generation stage (e.g., PRD generation) should complete within 3 minutes for a standard-sized input.
A full pipeline run from brief to stories should complete in under 15 minutes.
scalability: The Rust backend must be stateless to allow for horizontal scaling.
The system must be able to handle at least 20 concurrent pipeline executions without significant performance degradation.
The async architecture should efficiently manage I/O-bound tasks like calls to the Gemini API.
security: All external API keys (Gemini, Multica) must be stored in a secure vault (e.g., HashiCorp Vault) and not in code or configuration files.
All data in transit must be encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher.
User-provided briefs and all generated artifacts must be encrypted at rest.
Access to the pipeline API must be controlled via authenticated API tokens.

out_of_scope: A graphical user interface (GUI) for submitting briefs, monitoring pipeline progress, or editing artifacts.
Support for frontend code generation (e.g., React, Vue, HTML/CSS).
Real-time collaborative editing of generated documents.
Integration with version control systems (e.g., automatically creating pull requests in GitHub).
Integration with any issue tracker other than Multica.
raw_response.problem_statement: The traditional software development lifecycle is characterized by slow, manual, and error-prone handoffs between different roles. Product managers spend days writing detailed PRDs, which architects then manually translate into technical designs. This process often introduces inconsistencies. Developers then receive user stories that may be ambiguous or disconnected from the original intent. This friction leads to significant delays, rework, and a high cost of coordination, ultimately slowing down innovation.

risks.description: The quality and consistency of LLM-generated artifacts are insufficient, requiring extensive manual rework and defeating the purpose of automation.
risks.mitigation: Implement a multi-step generation process with self-correction loops. Use strict schema validation and few-shot prompting with high-quality examples to guide the LLM. Develop a 'human-in-the-loop' review step for the MVP.

risks.description: The cost of frequent, large-context Gemini API calls becomes prohibitively expensive as usage scales.
risks.mitigation: Implement aggressive caching for artifact generation. Optimize prompts to be as concise as possible. Introduce budget monitoring and alerting. Explore using smaller, cheaper models for less complex generation tasks.

risks.description: Changes in the Multica API break the integration, causing the final step of the pipeline to fail.
risks.mitigation: Build the Multica client behind a well-defined interface (adapter pattern) to isolate it from the core logic. Implement comprehensive integration tests against the Multica API and subscribe to their developer updates.

raw_response.solution_overview: Ensi introduces a fully automated, state-driven pipeline to address these challenges. A user submits a product brief, which triggers the Pipeline Finite State Machine (FSM). The FSM orchestrates a sequence of LLM agents, each responsible for a specific stage: Brief → PRD → Architecture → Stories → Code. A key feature is the structured output validation at each state transition, ensuring the quality and consistency of every artifact before the next stage begins. The system is built on a high-performance Rust backend, leverages the Gemini API for AI capabilities, and integrates with the Multica platform for seamless issue tracking and project management.

success_metrics.baseline: 40 hours (5 business days) for manual process.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Tracking the ratio of successful end-state runs versus failed-state runs in the pipeline logs.
success_metrics.target: 95% of pipeline runs complete successfully without entering a 'FAILED' state.

success_metrics.baseline: 15% of stories require significant clarification or changes after being picked up.
success_metrics.measurement_method: Surveying developers and tracking a custom 'Clarification Required' tag in Multica on Ensi-generated issues.
success_metrics.target: < 5% of stories generated by Ensi require clarification.

Free up time to focus on strategic planning, user research, and market analysis.

raw_response.executive_summary: Ensi is an AI-powered product development pipeline that automates and accelerates the software development lifecycle. By orchestrating a series of specialized LLM agents within a Finite State Machine (FSM), Ensi transforms a high-level product brief into a series of structured, validated artifacts: a Product Requirements Document (PRD), a technical architecture, user stories, and deployable boilerplate code. The MVP focuses on a Rust backend, Gemini API integration, and pushing user stories to the Multica issue tracking platform, aiming to significantly reduce manual effort and increase development velocity.

functional_requirements.description: The system must use a Finite State Machine (FSM) to manage the progression of a development task from one stage to the next, ensuring a sequential and controlled workflow.
The system must be able to generate structured artifacts for each stage of the pipeline: PRD, Architecture, User Stories, and Code.
The system must accept a product brief as initial input and validate the schema of each generated artifact before proceeding to the next stage.
The system must integrate with the Multica issue tracking platform to create new issues from the generated user stories.
The system must provide a command-line interface (CLI) for users to initiate pipelines and check their status.

non_functional_requirements.availability: The Ensi service will target 99.5% uptime during the MVP phase.
performance: P95 latency for PRD generation from a standard brief should be under 60 seconds.
P95 latency for end-to-end pipeline execution (Brief to Stories) should be under 5 minutes.
The system must handle at least 10 concurrent pipeline executions without significant performance degradation.
scalability: The backend service must be built on an asynchronous Rust framework (e.g., Tokio, Actix) to handle I/O-bound tasks efficiently.
The application should be containerized using Docker for horizontal scalability.
The FSM state should be persisted in a durable database (e.g., PostgreSQL) to allow for stateless service instances.
security: All external API keys (Gemini, Multica) must be stored in an encrypted secrets manager and not in source code.
Access to the pipeline execution service must be authenticated.
Input data (briefs) and generated artifacts should be treated as confidential and stored with encryption at rest.

out_of_scope: A graphical user interface (GUI). The MVP will be CLI-only.
Human-in-the-loop feedback and editing of artifacts mid-pipeline.
Support for programming languages other than Rust for code generation.
Integrations with version control systems (e.g., automatically creating feature branches).
Integrations with CI/CD systems.
raw_response.problem_statement: The traditional software development process is characterized by significant manual overhead, slow handoffs between teams (PM, Architecture, Engineering), and a high potential for inconsistencies. Translating a product brief into actionable engineering tasks is time-consuming, error-prone, and requires constant communication to bridge context gaps. This leads to longer development cycles, increased costs, and a risk of the final product deviating from the initial vision.

risks.description: The quality and consistency of LLM-generated artifacts may be low, requiring significant manual rework and undermining the value proposition.
risks.mitigation: Implement rigorous, schema-based validation for all outputs. Use advanced prompt engineering, including few-shot examples and chain-of-thought reasoning. Introduce a final manual approval gate before stories are pushed to Multica.

risks.description: Changes in external APIs (Gemini or Multica) could break the pipeline.
risks.mitigation: Create abstraction layers for all external API clients. Implement comprehensive integration tests against these APIs. Monitor API changelogs and have a plan for quick adaptation.

risks.description: The complexity of the FSM and agent interactions could lead to difficult-to-debug issues like race conditions or deadlocks.
risks.mitigation: Implement detailed structured logging for all state transitions and agent activities. Develop a robust suite of unit and integration tests for the FSM. Keep the state transitions simple and idempotent where possible.

raw_response.solution_overview: Ensi introduces a fully automated pipeline that ingests a product brief and systematically generates all necessary development artifacts. The core of Ensi is a Pipeline FSM written in Rust, which orchestrates specialized AI agents for each stage. Each agent leverages the Gemini API to perform its task (e.g., the 'PRD Agent' writes a detailed PRD). Crucially, the output of each stage is structurally validated against a predefined schema before the FSM transitions to the next state, ensuring quality and consistency. The final 'Stories Agent' generates user stories and pushes them directly into the Multica platform via API integration, creating a seamless flow from idea to backlog.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Timestamp logging at the start of the 'Generating PRD' state and the end of the 'Pushing Stories' state.
Surveying developers and tracking the percentage of Ensi-generated tickets that are started without requiring major edits to the title or acceptance criteria.
success_metrics.target: 80% of generated user stories are deemed 'ready for development' by an engineer without significant modification.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Monitoring and logging the final state of all initiated pipelines. (Success Rate = Successful Runs / Total Runs).
success_metrics.target: 95% of pipeline runs complete successfully without encountering an unrecoverable error.

acceptance_criteria: The system must securely authenticate with the Multica API using a configured API token.
After story generation is complete, the pipeline transitions to a 'Pushing Stories' state.
For each user story, a new issue is created in the specified Multica project.
The Multica issue title should be the user story title, and the description should contain the full story and its acceptance criteria.
user_stories.i_want: Ensi to automatically create issues in our Multica project for each generated user story
user_stories.so_that: they are seamlessly integrated into our existing sprint planning workflow.
user_stories.title: Push Stories to Multica

acceptance_criteria: A JSON schema is defined for each artifact type (PRD, Architecture, Stories).
After an artifact is generated by an LLM agent, it is parsed and validated against its corresponding schema.
If validation fails, the pipeline enters an 'Error' state and logs the validation errors.
The pipeline only proceeds to the next state if the current artifact passes validation.
user_stories.i_want: the system to validate the structure of each generated artifact at every stage
user_stories.so_that: I can trust the output is consistent, high-quality, and usable by the next stage.
user_stories.title: Validate Artifact Structure

acceptance_criteria: A CLI command `ensi status --id <pipeline_id>` is available.
The command outputs the current state of the FSM (e.g., 'Generating Architecture', 'Completed', 'Error').
If the pipeline is in an 'Error' state, the command outputs the associated error message.
The command returns a 'Not Found' error if the pipeline ID is invalid.
user_stories.i_want: to check the real-time status of a running pipeline using its ID
user_stories.so_that: I can monitor its progress without checking logs.
user_stories.title: Check Pipeline Status

acceptance_criteria: After stories are generated, the pipeline enters a 'Generating Code' state.
For each story, the system generates corresponding Rust files (e.g., `mod.rs`, `handlers.rs`).
The generated code should include function stubs, basic data structs, and TODO comments based on the acceptance criteria.
The generated code must be syntactically correct and compilable.
user_stories.i_want: Ensi to generate initial Rust boilerplate code for a user story
user_stories.so_that: I can avoid manual setup and focus on implementing the core logic.
user_stories.title: Generate Boilerplate Code

raw_response.executive_summary: Ensi is an AI-powered product development pipeline that automates and accelerates the software development lifecycle. By orchestrating a series of specialized LLM agents, Ensi transforms a high-level product brief into a series of structured, validated artifacts: a Product Requirements Document (PRD), a technical architecture, user stories, and boilerplate code. This MVP aims to validate the core pipeline's ability to significantly reduce manual effort, improve consistency, and decrease the time from idea to implementation.

functional_requirements.description: The system must orchestrate the flow of data and control between AI agents using a Finite State Machine. The pipeline progresses through discrete states: Brief Ingestion, PRD Generation, Architecture Generation, Story Generation, Code Generation.
The system must use specific AI agents to generate structured artifacts at each stage of the pipeline. This includes generating a PRD, a technical architecture document, user stories, and Rust code.
Each artifact generated by an AI agent must be validated against a predefined schema to ensure it is well-structured and contains all required information before proceeding to the next stage.
The system must be able to create new issues in the Multica platform based on the generated user stories, populating fields such as title, description, and acceptance criteria.

non_functional_requirements.availability: The Ensi API will maintain a 99.5% uptime, measured monthly.
performance: P95 latency for PRD generation from a 1000-word brief should be under 3 minutes.
P95 latency for code generation for a single story should be under 90 seconds.
The API endpoints for status checks and artifact retrieval must respond in under 500ms.
scalability: The Rust backend service must be stateless and horizontally scalable to handle at least 50 concurrent pipeline executions.
The system must be able to process product briefs up to 5,000 words in length.
The database schema must be designed to support millions of artifacts without performance degradation.
security: All communication with the Gemini API and Multica API must be over HTTPS (TLS 1.2+).
API keys and other secrets must be stored securely using a secret management service (e.g., HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager).
Input briefs and generated artifacts must be encrypted at rest using AES-256.
Access to the Ensi API will be controlled by API keys with rate limiting.

out_of_scope: A graphical user interface (GUI) for submitting briefs or reviewing artifacts. MVP is API-only.
Support for code generation in languages other than Rust.
Human-in-the-loop feedback and editing of artifacts within the system.
Direct integration with Git repositories for code commits.
Automated deployment of generated code.
raw_response.problem_statement: The traditional software development process is characterized by significant manual effort, time-consuming handoffs between roles (PM, Architect, Developer), and a high potential for misinterpretation of requirements. This leads to slow development cycles, inconsistent documentation, and increased costs. Developers spend valuable time on boilerplate code and clarifying ambiguous stories, while PMs and architects are bogged down in creating and maintaining detailed documentation, detracting from strategic work.

risks.description: The quality and consistency of LLM-generated artifacts may be low, requiring significant manual rework and undermining the product's value.
risks.mitigation: Implement rigorous, schema-based validation for all generated outputs. Use prompt engineering techniques like chain-of-thought and few-shot examples. Introduce a mandatory, lightweight human approval step at each stage of the pipeline for the MVP.

risks.description: The cost of frequent, large-context calls to the Gemini API could become prohibitively expensive as usage scales.
risks.mitigation: Implement a caching layer for similar requests. Optimize prompts to be as concise as possible. Use smaller, cheaper models for less complex tasks like summarization or formatting. Implement strict budget monitoring and alerting.

risks.description: The Multica API integration may be brittle or have low rate limits, causing failures in the final stage of the pipeline.
risks.mitigation: Develop a robust integration client with exponential backoff and retry logic. Implement a queueing system for Multica ticket creation to handle API downtime or rate limiting. Provide clear error reporting to the user if ticket creation fails.

risks.description: Development teams may be resistant to adopting a new, AI-driven workflow, preferring their existing manual processes.
risks.mitigation: Engage a pilot team early in the development process to gather feedback. Provide comprehensive documentation and training. Focus initial marketing on clear value propositions like 'eliminate boilerplate' and 'get clearer stories'.

raw_response.solution_overview: Ensi introduces a Finite State Machine (FSM) orchestrated pipeline of AI agents to automate artifact generation. A user submits a product brief, which triggers the pipeline. The 'PM Agent' generates a PRD, which, upon approval, is passed to the 'Architect Agent' to create a technical design. This design is then used by the 'Developer Agent' to break down the work into user stories and generate initial boilerplate code in Rust. Each stage's output is structurally validated to ensure quality and consistency. The pipeline integrates with the Multica issue tracking platform to automatically create tickets for the generated stories, seamlessly fitting into existing development workflows.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Timestamp difference between pipeline initiation and completion of the story generation stage, tracked within the system.
success_metrics.metric: Lead Time from Brief to Stories

success_metrics.measurement_method: System logs tracking the number of pipelines that reach the 'Complete' state versus the number that fail.
success_metrics.target: 80% of pipelines complete all stages without critical errors.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Surveys and interviews with pilot development teams comparing time logs for features developed with and without Ensi.
success_metrics.target: Reduce time spent on story refinement and boilerplate coding by 25%.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Tracking the number of unique API keys making successful pipeline initiation calls.
success_metrics.target: 2 internal engineering teams actively using Ensi for new feature development within 3 months of launch.

goals: Accelerate the time from concept to a ready-for-development backlog.
Focus more on user research and product strategy.
Ensure high-quality, consistent requirements documentation.
Improve alignment between product and engineering.
target_personas.name: Priya
pain_points: Spending days writing and refining detailed PRDs.
Ensuring engineering fully understands the requirements and business context.
Manual creation of tickets in the issue tracking system.
Keeping documentation consistent and up-to-date.

goals: Rapidly generate robust, scalable, and secure architecture proposals.
Ensure technical designs are directly and accurately derived from approved requirements.
Automate the creation of architecture diagrams and documentation.
Spend more time on complex design challenges and innovation.
target_personas.name: Alex
pain_points: Manually translating business requirements from a PRD into a technical design.
Repetitive work in designing common application components.
Documenting architectural decisions and ensuring they are followed.
Risk of design flaws due to misinterpreting the PRD.

goals: Receive clear, well-defined, and actionable user stories.
Start coding features immediately with pre-generated boilerplate.
Focus on writing core business logic rather than setup tasks.
Increase feature development velocity.
target_personas.name: Ben
pain_points: Receiving ambiguous or incomplete user stories.
Wasting time writing boilerplate code and setting up new services.
Context switching between documentation, issue trackers, and the IDE.
Delays caused by waiting for requirement clarifications.

raw_response.title: Ensi: AI-Powered Product Development Pipeline

acceptance_criteria: An API endpoint exists to accept a POST request with the product brief as a text payload.
A successful submission initiates the Pipeline FSM, starting in the 'PRD Generation' state.
The system returns a unique pipeline ID to track the request.
The submission fails with a clear error message if the brief is empty or malformed.
user_stories.i_want: to submit a product brief via an API endpoint
user_stories.so_that: I can kickstart the automated artifact generation process for a new feature.
user_stories.title: Initiate Pipeline with Product Brief

acceptance_criteria: The PM Agent processes the input brief and generates a JSON-based PRD.
The generated PRD must pass schema validation, including sections for problem statement, user personas, and functional requirements.
The pipeline status updates to 'Awaiting PRD Approval' upon successful generation.
The generated PRD is retrievable via an API endpoint using the pipeline ID.
user_stories.i_want: the system to automatically generate a structured PRD from my brief
user_stories.so_that: I can review and validate the detailed requirements quickly.
user_stories.title: Generate Structured PRD

acceptance_criteria: The Architect Agent processes an approved PRD.
The generated architecture document includes key components, data models, API endpoints, and technology stack recommendations.
The output must pass its corresponding schema validation.
The pipeline status updates to 'Awaiting Architecture Approval'.
user_stories.i_want: the system to generate a technical architecture document from an approved PRD
user_stories.so_that: I have a consistent and requirements-aligned starting point for the system design.
user_stories.title: Generate Technical Architecture

acceptance_criteria: The Developer Agent processes the approved architecture and PRD.
It generates a list of user stories in the standard 'As a..., I want..., so that...' format.
Each story includes detailed acceptance criteria.
The list of stories is retrievable via the API.
user_stories.i_want: the system to break down the architecture and PRD into actionable user stories
user_stories.so_that: I have a clear and ready-to-work backlog.
user_stories.title: Generate User Stories from Architecture

acceptance_criteria: After story generation, the system connects to the Multica API using pre-configured credentials.
A new issue is created in the specified Multica project for each user story.
The Multica issue title, description, and acceptance criteria are populated from the generated story.
The generated story in Ensi is updated with a link to the corresponding Multica ticket.
user_stories.i_want: the system to automatically create tickets in Multica for each generated user story
user_stories.so_that: work can be tracked in our existing project management tool without manual data entry.
user_stories.title: Create Tickets in Multica

acceptance_criteria: An API endpoint exists that accepts a pipeline ID.
The endpoint returns the current state of the FSM (e.g., 'Generating PRD', 'Awaiting Approval', 'Complete').
The endpoint also provides links to any artifacts that have been generated so far.
An error is returned if the pipeline ID is invalid.
user_stories.i_want: to be able to query the status of a pipeline run
user_stories.so_that: I can track its progress from brief to code.
user_stories.title: View Pipeline Status

acceptance_criteria: The Developer Agent takes a single user story and its architectural context as input.
It generates valid Rust code files, including main function, modules, and data structures (structs).
The generated code includes comments linking it back to the user story requirements.
The code is retrievable as a downloadable archive via the API.
user_stories.i_want: the system to generate initial Rust boilerplate code for a user story
user_stories.so_that: I can accelerate the implementation of the feature.
user_stories.title: Generate Boilerplate Rust Code

constraints: Limited time for initial project definition.
Reliance on existing documentation for background information.
Need for consensus among diverse stakeholder groups.
goals: To clearly define the core problem being addressed by the project.
To establish a common understanding among all project participants.
To provide a foundation for setting project objectives and success metrics.
non_goals: To design specific technical solutions.
To allocate resources or define project tasks.
To conduct detailed market research.
raw_response.problem_statement: The current system lacks a clear problem statement, leading to ambiguity in project scope and objectives.
success_metrics: Problem statement is approved by all key stakeholders.
Project team members can articulate the problem statement accurately.
Future project documentation consistently references the defined problem statement.
raw_response.target_audience: Project managers and stakeholders who need a clear understanding of project goals.
raw_response.title: test
